courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: Compressed foundations from the original Week 1: empirical motivation, heterogeneity in MPCs and balance sheets, and why representative-agent frameworks miss key policy mechanisms.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Reduced-form VARs, VMA representations, impulse responses, forecast error variance decompositions, and the mapping from reduced-form to structural parameters. The role of economic theory, timing assumptions, and institutional knowledge in achieving identification.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: Auclert-style decomposition of transmission channels, redistribution and exposure effects, and the intertemporal Keynesian cross.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Short-run (Cholesky) and long-run (Blanchard–Quah) zero restrictions. Sign restrictions as set identification: the algorithm of Rubio-Ramírez, Waggoner, and Zha (2010), interpretation of identified sets, and Uhlig (2005)'s rejection method. Narrative restrictions as economically motivated inequalities on shocks and responses.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: Transfers, taxes, government spending, fiscal multipliers, and distributional incidence in HANK environments.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Proxy variables as noisy measures of structural shocks. The SVAR-IV approach of Stock and Watson and Mertens and Ravn. Relevance and exogeneity conditions. Identification with high-frequency surprises. Comparing proxy-based identification with zero- and sign-restricted approaches, and discussing when different strategies can be fruitfully combined.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
topic: Hands-on Python: solving a canonical one-asset HANK model.
description: Guided implementation of the core building blocks of the canonical model, sequence-space intuition, and interpretation of model-based impulse responses and policy counterfactuals.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Practical session led by TA. Estimating reduced-form VARs and computing impulse responses. Implementing Cholesky and sign-restricted identification. Proxy SVAR estimation of monetary policy shocks using high-frequency instruments. Replication of selected results from the empirical literature.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: From TANK to THANK: analytical frameworks that capture key heterogeneity channels while remaining transparent and portable.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: The Jordà (2005) LP estimator as a direct, regression-based alternative to VARs. Robustness to misspecification. HAC inference, confidence intervals, and smooth local projections. LP vs. VAR: the Plagborg-Møller and Wolf (2021) equivalence result and its implications for applied work.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: Policy intuition, amplification and redistribution, forward guidance, and comparison with quantitative HANK results.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Identification via external instruments in local projections. The connection between LP–IV and SVAR–IV assumptions. Invertibility, weak-instrument concerns, and the role of inference in comparing dynamic causal estimates across methods. State-dependent impulse responses and nonlinear extensions.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
topic: Fiscal policy in THANK and HANK.
description: Comparing tax, transfer, and spending experiments across tractable and quantitative models.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: The curse of dimensionality in dynamic systems. Factor-augmented methods (FAVARs), penalized estimation, shrinkage, and recent tools for inference on impulse responses in large information environments. Large SVARs, dimensionality reduction, and computational issues.

courseName: Monetary and Fiscal Policy in HANK
description: When HANK is worth the extra complexity, when tractable models are enough, and how the framework can be used by researchers and policy institutions.

courseName: Causal Inference for Macroeconomic Shocks
description: Practical session led by TA. LP and LP–IV estimation of impulse responses. Comparing LP–IV and SVAR–IV estimates on the same dataset. State-dependent local projections. Working with richer datasets and factor-augmented specifications. Replication of selected empirical results.
